ref.title: Lise Meitner: A Life in Physics
ref.author: Ruth Lewin Sime

feed_card.name: Lise Meitner
feed_card.essence: Austrian-Swedish physicist who interpreted the experiment that proved uranium nuclei could split, then refused to help build the bomb her theory made possible.
teasers: Smuggled out of Berlin with ten marks
Did the math in the snow

sections.content: Lise Meitner was an Austrian-born physicist who spent thirty years building nuclear physics in Berlin, fled Nazi Germany at age fifty-nine with two suitcases and a borrowed diamond ring, and five months later worked out the calculation showing that a uranium atom can be split in two. The 1944 Nobel Prize for that discovery went to her chemist collaborator alone.

content.image_caption: Lise Meitner with Otto Hahn at the Kaiser Wilhelm Institute for Chemistry in Berlin-Dahlem, 1913. They worked together for thirty years.

content.quote: I will have nothing to do with a bomb.
Life need not be easy, provided only that it is not empty.
I love physics with all my heart. It is a kind of personal love, as one has for a person to whom one is grateful for many things.
You must not blame us scientists for the use which war technicians have put our discoveries.

content.known_for: Theoretical interpretation of nuclear fission; co-discovery of protactinium

sections.content: The atomic age opened with a calculation done on the back of an envelope during a walk in the snow. Lise Meitner was the physicist who did it. In December 1938 her old Berlin partner Otto Hahn sent her a letter from Germany describing a chemical result he could not explain: when uranium was bombarded with neutrons, the residue contained barium, an element about half the weight of uranium. Meitner, six months into exile in Sweden, walked into the woods with her nephew Otto Frisch and worked out what had happened. The uranium nucleus had broken in two. The lost mass had become energy, in the amount Einstein's equation predicted, about 200 million electron-volts per atom. The chemistry was Hahn's. The interpretation was hers. The 1944 Nobel Prize in Chemistry went to Hahn alone, with no mention of her. Meitner is also the scientist who, when later asked to come to Los Alamos and help build the weapon her theory made possible, said no. Her thirty-year partnership with Hahn, her escape from Berlin in 1938, and her refusal to enter weapons work make her one of the rare twentieth-century physicists whose career can be read both as a triumph of theory and as a study of what scientific credit gets withheld and from whom.

content.title: Tutored at Home While Vienna Barred Women From School
content.body: Vienna did not allow women into the Gymnasium until 1897. Meitner finished her primary schooling at fourteen, then spent eight years studying with a private tutor at her father's expense. When the university opened to women, she sat the Matura with a year of work compressed into the gap and entered the University of Vienna at twenty-three. She finished her physics doctorate in 1906, the second woman in the university's history to do so. By the time her career began, most of her male contemporaries had been working in laboratories for a decade.

content.title: Crosses to Berlin and Meets Hahn
content.body: Meitner moved to Berlin in 1907 to attend Max Planck's lectures. Planck had never accepted a woman into his lecture course; he made an exception. Within a year she was his assistant. She met the young chemist Otto Hahn at the Chemical Institute and began a working partnership that would last until 1938. For her first years there she was not allowed into the main laboratory because women were banned from the building. She worked from a converted carpenter's workshop with a separate entrance, and was forbidden to use the toilet, which was reserved for men.
content.image_caption: Meitner as a doctoral candidate in Vienna, 1906. She would leave for Berlin the following year.
content.image_attribution: Courtesy Churchill Archives Centre, Cambridge; Wikimedia Commons

content.title: Nurses X-Ray Patients on the Russian Front
content.body: When the First World War broke out, Meitner volunteered as an X-ray technician for the Austrian army and was posted to military hospitals near the Russian front. She had no medical training; she taught herself the equipment. She saw a year of shrapnel wounds, gas burns, and amputations before returning to Berlin in late 1916. Her later refusal to work on weapons traced back, in her own letters, to what she had seen of war up close.

content.title: Becomes Germany's First Woman Professor of Physics
content.body: In 1922 the University of Berlin accepted her Habilitation thesis; in 1926 she was appointed Extraordinary Professor of Physics. She was the first woman in Germany to hold a physics chair. The Berlin newspapers reported it as a story about the female sex. Meitner read the coverage and kept working. By the early 1930s she headed the physics section of the Kaiser Wilhelm Institute for Chemistry and was running her own group on radioactive elements.

content.title: Leaves Berlin by Train With Ten Marks
content.body: After the Anschluss in March 1938, Austrian Jews became German citizens overnight and lost their protection from the racial laws. Meitner stayed in Berlin too long; her colleagues begged her to leave; she kept hoping a position abroad would come together. On 13 July she boarded an evening train to the Dutch border with the chemist Dirk Coster, who had crossed into Germany to escort her. She carried two small suitcases of summer clothing and ten marks. Hahn pressed his mother's diamond ring into her hand in case she needed to bribe a border guard. She crossed without being stopped. She would not see Germany again for ten years.
content.image_caption: Meitner and Hahn at the Kaiser Wilhelm Institute around 1928, a decade before the separation.

content.title: Christmas Walk in Kungälv
content.body: Meitner spent that first Christmas of exile at a small hotel north of Gothenburg, where her nephew Otto Frisch came to join her. She had a letter from Hahn in her pocket. He and Strassmann had bombarded uranium with neutrons and found barium in the residue, and could not explain it. Hahn asked her what physics had to say. On Christmas Eve she and Frisch walked into the snowy woods (Frisch on skis, Meitner on foot) and sat down on a fallen tree trunk. She sketched a uranium nucleus as a wobbling droplet. Frisch took the figures. By the time they walked back, they had calculated that the droplet could split into two, that the lost mass corresponded to about 200 million electron-volts per atom by Einstein's equation, and that this was what Hahn had seen.

content.title: Refuses the Manhattan Project
content.body: Frisch coined the word "fission" in their February 1939 paper in Nature. Within months, Leo Szilard had read it and started writing the letter that, with Einstein's signature, would push Roosevelt toward the Manhattan Project. In 1943 the British asked Meitner to come to Los Alamos. Her answer was four words: "I will have nothing to do with a bomb." She spent the war in Stockholm, working in a laboratory where Manne Siegbahn gave her almost no support and her name was rarely spoken. When she heard the news from Hiroshima on 6 August 1945, she went for a walk of five hours and refused to comment to the press.

content.title: The Liquid-Drop Interpretation of Nuclear Fission
content.body: Before Meitner and Frisch's paper, the nucleus was thought of as a brittle, granular thing. A neutron could chip off a proton or kick out an alpha particle, but the idea that a heavy nucleus could split into two pieces of roughly equal size was considered physically absurd. Hahn himself wrote to Meitner that the barium result "contradicts all previous experience of nuclear physics." Meitner's answer drew on a model first proposed by George Gamow and Niels Bohr: treat the nucleus as a charged liquid drop, with surface tension holding it together and electrostatic repulsion pushing it apart. In a uranium nucleus those two forces nearly cancel. A small disturbance is enough to let the drop elongate, pinch in the middle, and break into two smaller drops. Meitner then ran the energy ledger. The two daughter nuclei together weigh about a fifth of a proton mass less than the uranium nucleus and the neutron that struck it. By E=mc², that missing mass should appear as about 200 million electron-volts of kinetic energy carried away by the fragments. Frisch confirmed the prediction experimentally within weeks: an ionisation chamber in Copenhagen registered exactly the giant pulses the calculation predicted. The paper in Nature, dated 11 February 1939, took up two pages. It changed nuclear physics overnight.

sections.content: Meitner was shy in the way that very precise people often are. She practised the piano daily, wrote long letters to friends in German even after forty years in Sweden, and called Hahn "my dear Hähnchen" until he abandoned her in 1938 and for some years afterward. Einstein called her "our Madame Curie"; she found the comparison embarrassing. She refused honorary doctorates that she felt she had not earned. After 1938 she became colder in correspondence with Hahn, partly because he kept publishing the fission work without crediting her, and partly because he wrote to her after the war that ordinary Germans had suffered as much as the Jews. She wrote back: "You all worked for Nazi Germany, and never even tried passive resistance. Of course, to ease your conscience you helped here and there a persecuted person; but millions of innocent people were murdered, and no one protested." The letter went unsent for years; she found it among her papers after his death. Late in life she said she had loved physics the way one loves a person.

content.body: Meitner's name was almost forgotten in the decades after the war. Hahn collected the Nobel medal in 1946 and did not share it. Histories of fission written in the 1950s described Meitner as Hahn's assistant. The shift began with Ruth Lewin Sime's 1996 biography Lise Meitner: A Life in Physics, which drew on the unpublished Hahn-Meitner correspondence at the Churchill Archives Centre in Cambridge and showed in letter after letter that the physical interpretation of fission had been hers. By the 2000s the consensus had moved; today most physics textbooks credit Hahn and Strassmann with the chemistry and Meitner and Frisch with the physics. The Nobel committee has never formally addressed the omission. Her epitaph, written by her nephew Frisch, reads: "Lise Meitner: a physicist who never lost her humanity."
content.present_day_impact: Element 109 was named meitnerium in 1997, twenty-nine years after her death. It is one of only two elements named after a non-mythological woman, the other being curium. The Hahn-Meitner Institute in Berlin, originally named for Hahn alone, was renamed in 1959 to include her.

content.question: What was Meitner's specific contribution to the discovery of nuclear fission in December 1938?
options: She ran the chemistry experiment that detected barium in uranium residue
She supplied the physical interpretation that explained Hahn's chemical result
She built the neutron source used in Berlin
She first proposed that uranium could be split, before any data existed
content.explanation: Hahn and Strassmann did the chemistry in Berlin and detected the barium. Meitner, in exile in Sweden, used the liquid-drop model and Einstein's equation to explain why barium appeared and to predict the energy released. The chemistry alone could not say what had physically happened to the nucleus.

content.question: Why did Meitner have to leave Germany in 1938 even though she had converted to Lutheranism in 1908?
options: The Nazi racial laws classified her as Jewish by ancestry regardless of religion
Her Lutheran conversion was not legally recognised in Germany
She had given up German citizenship years earlier
Her Austrian passport was revoked after the Anschluss
content.explanation: The Nuremberg Laws defined Jewishness by descent, not faith. Meitner's Austrian citizenship had partly shielded her until March 1938, when Germany annexed Austria and she became subject to those laws. The conversion she had made thirty years earlier was irrelevant.

content.question: What model of the atomic nucleus did Meitner use to explain fission?
options: The plum-pudding model
The Bohr planetary model with electron shells
The liquid-drop model, treating the nucleus as a charged droplet
A quark-based model derived from the Standard Model
content.explanation: The liquid-drop model, developed by Gamow and Bohr, treats the nucleus as a drop held together by surface tension and pushed apart by electrostatic repulsion. For uranium those forces nearly balance, so a small disturbance can elongate the drop and split it in two. The Standard Model and quarks were not formulated until decades later.

content.question: Why did the 1944 Nobel Prize in Chemistry go to Otto Hahn alone, with no share for Meitner?
options: She had not contributed to the original Berlin experiments
The Nobel committee judged that exiled scientists could not be considered
A combination of disciplinary boundaries, wartime isolation, and the political situation of an exiled physicist
She declined the prize in writing
content.explanation: Historians of science point to several factors. The Chemistry committee defined the discovery as chemistry, not physics. Meitner was in exile and effectively invisible to the Stockholm committee. Hahn omitted her from the original paper. The omission has been studied as one of the clearest cases of Nobel misattribution in the twentieth century.

content.question: Roughly how much energy did Meitner calculate would be released per uranium atom split?
options: About 1 electron-volt
About 200 electron-volts
About 200 million electron-volts
About 200 billion electron-volts
content.explanation: The mass difference between the uranium nucleus and the two fragments works out to about 0.2 atomic mass units. Multiplied by c² via Einstein's equation, that gives roughly 200 million electron-volts (200 MeV) per fission, an order of magnitude more than chemical reactions release per atom. This is what Frisch confirmed experimentally weeks later.

content.question: What did Meitner answer when invited to join the Manhattan Project at Los Alamos in 1943?
options: She accepted but withdrew when she saw the lab conditions
She agreed only to advisory work, not weapons design
"I will have nothing to do with a bomb."
She demanded that Hahn join her as a condition
content.explanation: Her refusal is quoted in Sime's biography (page 305). Meitner had seen the wounds of the First World War as a front-line X-ray nurse, and her opposition to weapons work was settled long before. After Hiroshima, she went for a long walk and declined to speak to reporters.

content.question: What is the present-day form of recognition that Meitner did not live to see?
options: A posthumous Nobel Prize awarded in 1971
Element 109 was named meitnerium in 1997
The Hahn-Meitner Institute was founded under that name in 1990
A Royal Society medal was given in her name in 1980
content.explanation: Meitnerium, atomic number 109, was synthesised at GSI Darmstadt in 1982 and officially named for Meitner in 1997, twenty-nine years after her death. The Hahn-Meitner Institute was named for both as early as 1959, while she was still alive. There is no posthumous Nobel.

content.question: Which of the following best captures the historical lesson of Meitner's omission from the 1944 Nobel?
options: Nobel committees are blind to personal politics
Disciplinary boundaries and the position of an exiled woman scientist together produced the omission
Hahn deliberately fabricated his role in the discovery
The committee corrected the error within the decade
content.explanation: Hahn did real chemistry; he was not a fraud. The omission emerged from a combination of forces: the prize was categorised as chemistry, Meitner was an exile cut off from the Stockholm community, and her work was published separately because joint publication with Berlin was impossible. The committee has never reversed the decision.

sections.content: The Vienna of Meitner's youth was a city that produced Freud, Wittgenstein, Schoenberg, Klimt, and Boltzmann, and that barred women from secondary school until 1897 and from medical study until 1900. The Berlin she moved to in 1907 was the world capital of physics, with Planck, Einstein, Nernst, and von Laue within walking distance of each other, but the Chemical Institute where she worked kept its toilet closed to women. After 1933 the Kaiser Wilhelm Institute lost about a third of its staff to dismissals and emigration in the first wave of Nazi racial laws. Meitner kept her position because she was Austrian. After the Anschluss in March 1938 that protection vanished, and the situation for a Jewish scientist in Berlin moved within weeks from precarious to lethal. The Sweden she fled to was officially neutral but politically uncomfortable for refugee scientists; Manne Siegbahn, who ran the Nobel Institute in Stockholm, kept her on a small stipend and gave her little equipment, partly out of personal dislike and partly because Swedish defense work was classified. Meitner did her best work of the postwar period in a borrowed corner of someone else's laboratory.

Meitner is not a saint and she did not present herself as one. The sharpest charge against her is that she stayed in Berlin too long. Friends had urged her to leave from 1933 onward; she kept hoping for a position abroad and underestimated, until the Anschluss, how fast the situation would deteriorate. Some of her former students made it out years earlier; some did not get out at all. She continued working at the Kaiser Wilhelm Institute through 1938 while Jewish colleagues were being dismissed, and she did not publicly protest those dismissals. After the war she said, in private letters, that her own silence in those years was the thing she could not forgive herself for. There is also the matter of Hahn. Meitner kept the working relationship going by letter even after he had failed to credit her in the December 1938 paper, and some historians read this as professional pragmatism shading into self-effacement. The 1945 letter that finally said what she thought ("you all worked for Nazi Germany") she never sent. Her humanity is in the record, and so is the record of her hesitations.

content.label: Sime RL. Lise Meitner: A Life in Physics. University of California Press, 1996.
Meitner L, Frisch OR. Disintegration of Uranium by Neutrons: A New Type of Nuclear Reaction. Nature 1939;143:239–240.
Hahn O, Strassmann F. Über den Nachweis und das Verhalten der bei der Bestrahlung des Urans mittels Neutronen entstehenden Erdalkalimetalle. Naturwissenschaften 1939;27:11–15.
The Papers of Lise Meitner, Churchill Archives Centre, Cambridge (MTNR collection)
Frisch OR. Lise Meitner, 1878–1968. Biographical Memoirs of Fellows of the Royal Society 1970;16:405–420.
American Physical Society: December 1938, Discovery of Nuclear Fission
